key_points: Poverty is a situation in which a person is unable to get the minimum basic necessities of life like food, clothing, and shelter.
In India, poverty is measured using the 'Poverty Line,' which is based on income or consumption levels.
A minimum calorie intake is used to determine the poverty line: 2400 calories per person per day in rural areas and 2100 calories in urban areas.
The Poverty Line is higher in urban areas monetarily because the cost of essential goods is higher in cities.
Social Exclusion and Vulnerability are two important ways to look at poverty beyond just income.
Social groups most vulnerable to poverty are Scheduled Castes (SC) and Scheduled Tribes (ST). Economic groups include agricultural laborers and urban casual laborers.
The government uses a two-pronged strategy: Promotion of economic growth and Targeted anti-poverty programs.
Important schemes include MGNREGA (100 days of guaranteed work), PMRY, and SGSY.
formulas: Poverty Line (India) = Cost of Minimum Calorie Requirement + Basic Non-food Expenses
World Bank Standard: living on less than $1.90 per day.
examples: Social Exclusion: A poor person living only with other poor people, excluded from using the same facilities as better-off people.
Vulnerability: A person who has a higher risk of becoming poor if there is a flood or if they lose their job.
Inter-state disparity: Bihar and Odisha have the highest poverty rates, while Kerala and Punjab have the lowest.
Poorest of the poor: Women, elderly people, and female infants within a poor family who are denied equal resources.
Targeted Scheme: A village laborer getting work through the MGNREGA scheme to earn wages.
exam_tips: Remember that calorie needs are higher in rural areas because people do more physical work.
Identify the organization that conducts poverty surveys: NSSO (National Sample Survey Organisation).
Know the difference between SC/ST (social groups) and Agricultural/Casual laborers (economic groups).
Kerala reduced poverty by investing in 'Human Resources' (Education/Health).
West Bengal reduced poverty through 'Land Reforms'.
